screenshot_note: Puppeteer launched but remote navigation timed out — code review only
theme: Four items fixed, one partial fix regressed, six new findings — the office is tightening up

fixed_since_last_review.title: lone-survivor-label raised from 0.45rem to 0.6rem — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: Office.jsx:1586 — style now reads `fontSize: '0.6rem'`. Matches wb-task-count. Legibility restored.

fixed_since_last_review.title: isMidnight is now a separate check, not an alias — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: Office.jsx:874 — `const isMidnight = hour >= 0 && hour <= 4`. No longer equals isNight. The star field now only fires during true midnight hours, not the full 8PM–4AM window. Semantically correct.

fixed_since_last_review.title: WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES moved to module scope — Partially Fixed (see regression below)
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: App.jsx:39-41 — constant now at module scope. The repeated inline allocation is gone. However, the worker color customization was dropped in the process (see 'Worker confetti loses its colors' finding).

fixed_since_last_review.title: WorkerCard redundant filter removed — Fixed
fixed_since_last_review.how_fixed: App.jsx:316 — `activityEntries.slice(-5).reverse()` with no preceding `.filter(e => e.worker === worker.name)`. The dead allocating filter is gone.

ideas.title: Worker Confetti Lost Its Colors in the Module-Scope Fix
ideas.description: App.jsx:39-41 and 1174-1178 — WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES was correctly moved to module scope to fix the per-render allocation, but the worker color personalization was silently dropped. The old inline version applied `style={{ '--i': i, ...(i < 18 ? { background: workerConfetti.color } : {}) }}`. The new module-scope version only sets `'--i'`. The parent `.confetti-layer` div (line 1175) has no `style={{ '--worker-color': workerConfetti.color }}`, and there is no CSS rule for `--worker-color` in App.css. Result: worker confetti now looks identical to vibe confetti — every worker ships with the same generic purple/green/amber pieces regardless of role color. This is a regression: Maya's shipping was once recognizable by purple pieces; Carlos's by teal. Fix option A (simplest): on the `confetti-layer` div, add `style={{ '--worker-color': workerConfetti?.color }}`, then in CSS add `.confetti-layer .confetti-piece:nth-child(-n+18) { background: var(--worker-color, var(--accent)); }`. Fix option B (clean): re-inline only the color override. WHY: the personalization is what makes the worker confetti emotionally distinct from the vibe burst. Without it, the feature loses half its meaning and WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES is just a renamed clone of CONFETTI_PIECES.

ideas.title: Conference Table Last-Huddle Label at 0.38rem — Sub-pixel Legibility
ideas.description: Office.jsx:1002 — `style={{ opacity: 0.5, fontSize: '0.38rem', textAlign: 'center', marginTop: '2px' }}`. At 0.38rem ≈ 6px, this is below the 0.6rem floor we've established across two previous reviews. This label shows the last-huddle summary ('Last: Sofia & Maya · 3m ago') when the conference table is idle. At 6px with 50% opacity it is not legible to any human without a microscope. Fix: raise to `0.6rem` and increase opacity to 0.65. WHY: the last-huddle label is a storytelling moment — it says 'the team just met, something happened'. At 6px it contributes nothing and reads as a broken UI artifact.

ideas.title: isNight Is Declared But Never Used — Dead Variable
ideas.description: Office.jsx:873 — `const isNight = (hour >= 0 && hour <= 4) || hour >= 20`. The isMidnight fix in review #89 correctly separated isMidnight to `hour >= 0 && hour <= 4`. But isNight itself is never referenced in the JSX or logic — isMidnight (883), isDaytime (875), and isGoldenHour (876) are the only downstream usages. isNight is dead. Fix: delete the isNight declaration. If a full-night concept is ever needed (8PM–4AM), it lives in one place with a clear name. WHY: dead variables are landmines for future contributors who see `isNight` and assume it affects rendering — then spend time tracing it to nothing. The fix is one deleted line.

ideas.title: isStandup Has a Dead Object-vs-String OR Clause
ideas.description: Office.jsx:1444 — `const isStandup = occ && standupWorkers.some(sw => sw.id === occ.worker.id || sw.id === occ.worker)`. The `|| sw.id === occ.worker` compares `sw.id` (a string like 'carlos') to `occ.worker` (a full worker object) — this is always false. The clause is dead code that never contributes to the result. It looks like the correct condition (`sw.id === occ.worker.id`) was accidentally duplicated with `.id` dropped from the right side. Fix: remove the dead clause — `standupWorkers.some(sw => sw.id === occ.worker.id)`. WHY: the existing `standupWorkers.includes(occ.worker)` at line 1572 already handles standup filtering by reference equality (which works because the same object references are preserved through the memoized arrays). Having a broken `.some()` check here that silently always returns the same result as the first condition is a correctness risk if the object references ever change.

ideas.title: idleWorkers Has a Permanently-True Filter Condition
ideas.description: Office.jsx:1219 — `const idleWorkers = useMemo(() => nonMgr.filter(w => w.status === 'idle' && !workingIds.has(w.id)), ...)`. `workingIds` is built from workers whose `status === 'working'`. A worker with `status === 'idle'` can never appear in `workingIds`. The `!workingIds.has(w.id)` guard is always `true` for every idle worker and allocates a Set lookup that never changes the result. Fix: simplify to `nonMgr.filter(w => w.status === 'idle')`. WHY: the redundant condition implies to future readers that a worker can somehow be both idle and working simultaneously — which would be a data integrity bug. Removing it makes the intent clear: idle workers are workers with status idle, full stop. Correctness by clarity.

ideas.title: Activity Log Filter Logic Duplicated — Two Places to Update
ideas.description: App.jsx:1367-1380 and 1424-1432 — The activity filter logic (switching on activityFilter for hires/completions/errors/system, then filtering by workerFilter) is written identically in two separate places: once to compute the count label and once to produce the entries array for ActivityLog. If a new filter category is added (e.g., 'warnings'), it must be added in both places, and they could drift. Fix: `const filteredEntries = useMemo(() => activityLog.filter(...).filter(...), [activityLog, activityFilter, workerFilter])` — use this memo for both the count display and the ActivityLog entries prop. WHY: single-source of truth for filter logic prevents silent divergence. Currently if the count label says '3 events (filtered)' but ActivityLog actually shows 4 entries (because someone only updated one path), trust in the UI collapses.

ideas.title: quoteBonus Wrap Still Has No Ceremony — Sixth Consecutive Review
ideas.description: App.jsx:1447 — `p >= BONUS_TAGLINES.length ? 1 : p + 1` wraps silently. The counter `{quoteBonus > 0 && quoteBonus < BONUS_TAGLINES.length && {quoteBonus}/{BONUS_TAGLINES.length}}` disappears at `quoteBonus === 8` with no acknowledgment. The user just saw `7/8`, clicked once more, and the counter vanished — replaced by nothing. Fix: when `p >= BONUS_TAGLINES.length`, set a localStorage flag `'footer-all-taglines-seen'`. On the reset tick, fire a subtle toast: `'✦ You found all of Bloberto's thoughts.'` in the same style as the completion toast but with golden border. After the flag is set, the ✦ sparkle renders permanently gold (not needing the twinkle state). WHY: sixth consecutive review. The tagline hunt is the only discovery mechanic in the footer. Its completion must land — not vanish. The pattern already exists: localStorage flags, toasts, and color-coded pills. This is assembly, not invention.

ideas.title: WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES and CONFETTI_PIECES Are Now Identical
ideas.description: App.jsx:35-41 — Both constants are `Array.from({ length: 30 }, (_, i) => <span key={i} className='confetti-piece' style={{ '--i': i }} />)`. They produce identical React element arrays with the same keys, classes, and styles. Since the color regression was introduced, the only difference is the variable name. Fix: delete `WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES` and use `CONFETTI_PIECES` everywhere — or alternatively, fix the color regression first (the 'must' finding above) and then WORKER_CONFETTI_PIECES can live as the colored variant. WHY: having two module-scope constants that render identically is dead inventory that confuses the reader into thinking they produce different visual output.

ideas.title: Ghost Desk Has No Nameplate — OOO Info Invisible Without Character Hover
ideas.description:
Office.jsx:1500-1509 — When `occ.ghost === true`, neither nameplate branch renders. Working workers get `desk__nameplate--active`, idle workers get `desk__nameplate--away`, but ghost (roster-only) workers get nothing. The only way to see OOO info is to hover the Character SVG itself, which shows the `ghost-ooo-bubble`. But the Character for ghost workers is tiny (16px in standup, 36px at desk) and mobile users on touch devices get no hover. Fix: add a ghost nameplate branch: `{occ.ghost &&
📵 {occ.worker.name.split(' ')[0]}
}`. Optionally append relative last-seen time from activityEntries. WHY: empty desks with no nameplate look broken, not intentional. A small '📵 Dave' nameplate communicates 'this person exists, they're just not here today' — a human story vs an empty slot.

ideas.title: Active Tag Filter Has No Indicator on Dashboard Tab
ideas.description: App.jsx:750, 1262-1287 — `selectedTag` state persists across tab switches. When a tag filter is active (e.g., 'Bug' selected), worker cards are dimmed on the dashboard. Switching to the Office tab and back keeps the filter alive, but the Dashboard tab button shows no indication. Fix: add a small dot or badge to the Dashboard tab when `selectedTag !== null`: `📊 Dashboard{selectedTag ? · : null}`. WHY: invisible application state causes confusion. A user returning from the office doesn't know why half their cards are greyed out. The dot takes 3 lines of CSS and one JSX conditional.

ideas.title: CLOCK_COMMENTS Are Aria-Label Only — Nobody Reads aria-label on a Clock
ideas.description: Office.jsx:1086-1092, 1127 — The WallClock has delightful vibe-aware comments: 'Time has no meaning during an incident.' / 'Do not look. It breaks the flow.' But they're buried in the `aria-label` attribute. On desktop they're invisible (no visible tooltip). On mobile they don't exist. Fix: when the user hovers the clock (add `onMouseEnter`/`onFocus` to the SVG, remove `pointerEvents: 'none'`), show a tiny speech bubble below the clock face with the CLOCK_COMMENTS text. The bubble disappears after 3s. The same pattern as Bloberto's speech bubble — already in the codebase. WHY: the clock comments are some of the best writing in the app. 'Still here? So am I. I am a clock. That is fine for me.' is the funniest line in the file. It deserves to be seen.

ideas.title: Error Contagion: Nearby Workers Briefly React When a Colleague Errors
ideas.description: Wild idea: when a worker transitions to `status === 'error'`, the Character component for other working/idle workers near them (computed by desk proximity — workers sharing a desk row in DESKS array) briefly shows an error-adjacent idle-micro-bubble. For idle workers: one of their existing `WORKER_ERROR_BUBBLES` entries plays for 1.5s. For working workers: their typing-dots stutter (add a brief CSS class `typing-dots--glitch` with shake keyframe). Bloberto gets the most expressive reaction: a new `on-fire` ambient quote fires immediately instead of waiting for the 45s interval. The whole thing is a 600ms ripple — subtle, never blocking. WHY: the office is currently siloed. Each character lives in their own bubble. When an error lands, only the erroring desk reacts. But in a real office, the sound of 'oh no' is heard across the floor. Three lines of CSS and one useEffect make the space feel alive and interdependent.

ideas.title: Office Whiteboard Has Persistent Doodles That Accumulate Over Sessions
ideas.description: Wild idea: the `wb-doodle` pre at the bottom of the whiteboard (Office.jsx:1401-1402) currently shows a fixed emoticon per vibe. Extend it: store a small doodle history array in localStorage (up to 5 entries). Each vibe transition appends the current doodle to the array. On mount, render the last 3 doodles stacked vertically at reduced opacity, with the current one at full opacity. The result: the whiteboard looks used — traces of previous vibes visible like erased chalk. On `slow-day`, add a 'Meeting at 3pm — do not erase' note that persists until vibe changes to 'on-fire' (when it gets crossed out with '🔥'). WHY: the whiteboard is the most visible piece of ambient storytelling in the office. It currently resets completely on every vibe change. Persistent marks make the office feel like a place that has history, not just a real-time dashboard with cartoon furniture.

_still_open_from_89.note: Sixth consecutive review. This finding survives because it's a delight gap, not a breakage — nothing forces it onto the P0 list.
